content.text: Emergency Patch Procedures and Communication

An emergency patch is a security fix deployed outside the normal release cycle because the severity or exploitability of the vulnerability does not permit waiting. The process differs from routine patching in two ways: the timeline is compressed (hours instead of days or weeks), and the communication obligations are more demanding because stakeholders need timely updates on a fast-moving situation. A team that has never rehearsed this process will improvise poorly under pressure.

attrs.alt: Emergency patch process: five phases (Detect, Assess, Patch, Deploy, Communicate) with a rollback decision point, and a communication timeline from T+0 through T+7 days

content.text: Activation Criteria

The emergency patch procedure is activated when a vulnerability meets one or more of the following criteria. These criteria are the trigger, not a recommendation to consider activation. Meeting any one criterion means the procedure starts immediately.
Known active exploit:
A working exploit for the vulnerability is publicly available or confirmed in the wild, regardless of the CVSSv3 score. Exploitability changes the risk profile faster than severity alone.
Effective severity CRITICAL:
CVSSv3 score 9.0 or above, with an exposure context that keeps it CRITICAL after contextual adjustment (internet-facing component, not dead code, not auth-gated).
Direct API or model inference path:

A HIGH or CRITICAL vulnerability in a component on the direct path from user API request to model call. Lateral movement vulnerabilities requiring a separate service to be compromised first do not qualify.
Data exposure confirmed or suspected:
Any indication the vulnerability could expose user conversation data, API keys, or system prompts, at any CVSSv3 score.

from dataclasses import dataclass
from enum import Enum

class ExposureContext(str, Enum):
    INTERNET_FACING = "internet_facing"
    AUTH_GATED      = "auth_gated"
    INTERNAL_ONLY   = "internal_only"
    DEAD_CODE       = "dead_code"

@dataclass
class EmergencyPatchTrigger:
    cve_id: str
    cvss_score: float
    has_active_exploit: bool
    exposure: ExposureContext
    on_inference_hot_path: bool
    data_exposure_suspected: bool

    def should_activate_emergency_procedure(self) -> bool:
        if self.has_active_exploit:
            return True
        if (self.cvss_score >= 9.0
                and self.exposure in (ExposureContext.INTERNET_FACING,
                                      ExposureContext.AUTH_GATED)):
            return True
        if (self.cvss_score >= 7.0
                and self.on_inference_hot_path
                and self.exposure == ExposureContext.INTERNET_FACING):
            return True
        if self.data_exposure_suspected:
            return True
        return False

    def urgency_tier(self) -> str:
        if self.has_active_exploit or self.data_exposure_suspected:
            return "immediate"       # stop everything, start now
        if self.cvss_score >= 9.0:
            return "same-shift"      # complete before current on-call shift ends
        return "next-business-day"   # still uses emergency process
Command Structure During an Emergency Patch

Emergency patches require a clear command structure to prevent two failure modes: decision paralysis (no one is empowered to approve the production deploy without a normal change review) and diffusion of responsibility (multiple engineers are working on it but no one owns the outcome). The command structure mirrors incident management: one IC who owns the timeline, specific role assignments, and a dedicated communication channel.

The security_reviewer role is separate from the patch_engineer role deliberately. The engineer who writes the fix is not in the best position to verify it fully closes the vulnerability — confirmation bias is real, and time pressure amplifies it. The security reviewer independently confirms that the patched version eliminates the vulnerable code path. For a dependency version bump, this means reading the upstream patch notes and confirming the specific CVE is addressed in the version being deployed.
Internal Communication Protocol

Internal communication during an emergency patch follows a structured protocol to ensure the right people have the information they need without flooding channels with noise. Updates go to three audiences: the patch team in the dedicated incident channel, engineering leadership in the management channel, and the customer-facing team two hours before customer notice is sent.
Audience
Channel
Cadence
Content
Patch team
Dedicated incident channel
Every 30 min or on phase change
Current phase, blockers, ETA, next step
Engineering leadership
#engineering-management
Assessment complete, production deploy, rollback
CVE severity, affected component, timeline, risk
Account team (AE/TAM)
#enterprise-success
2h before customer notice sent
Draft customer message, impact, talking points
Support team
#support-ops
At deploy start and completion
Expected ticket increase, FAQ for L1 agents
Customer Disclosure Communication

Enterprise customers must be notified of security patches that affect them, even when the patch was successful and no customer data was exposed. The notification demonstrates operational transparency and gives the customer's security team information for their own compliance records. A customer who learns about a security vulnerability from a public CVE database rather than their vendor has already had their trust damaged.

from dataclasses import dataclass
from datetime import datetime

@dataclass
class CustomerSecurityNotice:
    cve_id: str
    severity: str
    affected_component: str
    patch_version: str
    deployed_at: datetime
    customer_impact: str
    customer_action_required: bool
    customer_action_detail: str | None

    def subject_line(self) -> str:
        return (
            f"Security Notice: {self.severity.upper()} vulnerability patched "
            f"-- {self.affected_component} ({self.cve_id})"
        )

    def body(self) -> str:
        lines = [
            f"We are writing to inform you that we have patched a {self.severity} "
            f"security vulnerability ({self.cve_id}) in {self.affected_component}.",
            "",
            f"The patch was deployed on {self.deployed_at.strftime('%Y-%m-%d at %H:%M UTC')}.",
            "",
            f"Customer impact: {self.customer_impact}",
        ]
        if self.customer_action_required:
            lines += ["", "Action required:", self.customer_action_detail or "(see attached)"]
        else:
            lines += ["", "No action is required from your team."]
        lines += [
            "",
            "Questions? Contact your account executive or open a ticket tagged 'security'.",
        ]
        return "\n".join(lines)

# customer_action_required is False for server-side patches (Anthropic deploys the fix).
# It is True for SDK vulnerabilities the customer must upgrade themselves.

The customer_action_required distinction between server-side and SDK vulnerabilities is critical. A notice that says "no action required" when the customer must actually upgrade their SDK version is a compliance failure for the customer's security team and will generate follow-up support tickets when the customer's security scanner flags the outdated SDK version. Server-side patches need notification only; SDK patches need notification plus upgrade guidance.
Post-Patch Review Requirements

Every emergency patch requires a post-patch review within 48 hours. The review is lighter than a full incident postmortem but covers the same structural questions: what triggered the emergency, whether the process worked as designed, and what changes would prevent either the vulnerability or the process failure from recurring.

from dataclasses import dataclass
from datetime import datetime

@dataclass
class PostPatchReview:
    cve_id: str
    detection_to_assessment_hours: float
    assessment_to_staging_hours: float
    staging_to_production_hours: float
    total_elapsed_hours: float
    rollback_occurred: bool
    rollback_reason: str | None
    process_worked_as_designed: bool
    process_gaps: list[str]
    detection_gap: str | None   # was it found by scanning, or external report?
    corrective_actions: list[str]
    reviewed_by: str
    completed_at: datetime

    def sla_met(self) -> bool:
        return self.total_elapsed_hours <= 24.0

    def summary(self) -> str:
        status = "MET" if self.sla_met() else f"MISSED by {self.total_elapsed_hours - 24:.1f}h"
        gaps = "\n  - ".join(self.process_gaps) if self.process_gaps else "None"
        actions = "\n  - ".join(self.corrective_actions)
        return (
            f"Post-Patch Review: {self.cve_id}\n"
            f"SLA: {status} (total: {self.total_elapsed_hours:.1f}h)\n"
            f"Rollback: {'Yes -- ' + (self.rollback_reason or '') if self.rollback_occurred else 'No'}\n"
            f"Process gaps:\n  - {gaps}\n"
            f"Corrective actions:\n  - {actions}"
        )

The detection_gap field captures whether the team's own scanning caught the vulnerability or whether they learned about it from an external source. An external detection means the 6-hour production scan did not surface the issue before it was public knowledge. If this recurs, the scanning coverage, interval, or CVE database feed needs investigation. The post-patch review is also the mechanism for identifying whether the emergency was avoidable: a vulnerability in a dependency version the team should have upgraded 30 days ago is a process failure, not just a security event.
The Abbreviated Testing Protocol in Detail

Running a full regression suite before a production deploy takes 30-90 minutes depending on the codebase. Against a 24-hour emergency window where assessment, fix acquisition, staging deploy, and monitoring each consume time, a full suite is not feasible for a CRITICAL patch. The abbreviated protocol covers only the failure modes most likely to be introduced by a dependency bump: broken authentication, broken API surface, and unexpected error log noise. Everything else is accepted as a conscious risk to be validated immediately after production deployment.

from dataclasses import dataclass
from enum import Enum

class TestResult(str, Enum):
    PASS    = "pass"
    FAIL    = "fail"
    SKIPPED = "skipped"   # not applicable to this patch type

@dataclass
class AbbreviatedTestGate:
    name: str
    description: str
    applies_to: list[str]   # component types this gate applies to
    blocking: bool           # True = must pass before production deploy

EMERGENCY_TEST_GATES = [
    AbbreviatedTestGate(
        name="unit_tests_changed_component",
        description="Run unit tests for the patched component only (not full suite)",
        applies_to=["all"],
        blocking=True,
    ),
    AbbreviatedTestGate(
        name="auth_path_test",
        description="Valid + invalid token scenarios; confirm 401 on bad token",
        applies_to=["auth_library", "http_client", "sdk"],
        blocking=True,
    ),
    AbbreviatedTestGate(
        name="api_smoke_test",
        description="10 requests across all major endpoint types; no 5xx responses",
        applies_to=["all"],
        blocking=True,
    ),
    AbbreviatedTestGate(
        name="model_call_end_to_end",
        description="Single Claude API call succeeds with expected response structure",
        applies_to=["sdk", "serialisation", "container_image"],
        blocking=True,
    ),
    AbbreviatedTestGate(
        name="log_noise_check",
        description="No new ERROR or CRITICAL log lines within 5 min of staging deploy",
        applies_to=["all"],
        blocking=True,
    ),
    AbbreviatedTestGate(
        name="streaming_parse_test",
        description="Streaming response parses all event types correctly",
        applies_to=["sdk", "serialisation"],
        blocking=True,
    ),
    AbbreviatedTestGate(
        name="full_regression",
        description="Full test suite (run post-production-deploy, not pre)",
        applies_to=["all"],
        blocking=False,   # non-blocking: runs after prod deploy
    ),
]

def applicable_gates(component_type: str) -> list[AbbreviatedTestGate]:
    return [
        g for g in EMERGENCY_TEST_GATES
        if "all" in g.applies_to or component_type in g.applies_to
    ]

def blocking_gates(component_type: str) -> list[AbbreviatedTestGate]:
    return [g for g in applicable_gates(component_type) if g.blocking]

The full_regression gate is non-blocking: it is scheduled to run immediately after production deployment, not before. If it catches a regression, the rollback plan is already in place and the monitoring window is active. Running it post-deploy rather than pre-deploy saves 30-90 minutes while accepting the risk that a non-critical regression reaches production briefly before being detected and rolled back. This trade-off is explicit and must be documented in the emergency patch operation record.
Bypassing Normal Change Management

Most production environments require a change management review before deployment — a CAB (change advisory board) review, a staging sign-off from QA, or at minimum a two-person approval in the deployment pipeline. Emergency patches must bypass some of these gates. How this bypass is structured determines whether it creates acceptable risk or a compliance violation.
Pre-authorised emergency change type:

The safest model defines an "emergency security patch" change type in the change management system that has pre-approved bypass authority. The IC can approve the deploy without a CAB review, but the approval is logged automatically against the change record. The bypass is authorised, not invisible.
Post-hoc review obligation:

Every bypassed change management gate must be reviewed within 48 hours in the post-patch review. The review confirms that the bypass was justified by the emergency criteria and that the abbreviated testing was appropriate for the change.
Approval authority chain:

The IC must have explicit approval authority for production deployment. If the IC is a staff engineer who normally requires a tech lead sign-off, the emergency patch procedure grants temporary approval authority. This authority must be defined in the procedure, not improvised during the event.
Audit trail completeness:

Bypassing a gate does not mean the gate disappears from the record. Every gate that was bypassed must be documented: which gate, who authorised the bypass, the justification, and the post-hoc review outcome. Compliance auditors review the audit trail specifically for gates that were not followed.
Handling a Patch That Requires Service Downtime

Most dependency patches can be deployed with zero downtime using a rolling deployment: new instances start with the patched version while old instances continue serving traffic, and traffic shifts over as new instances become healthy. But some patches — particularly those affecting the container base image or a library that cannot be hot-swapped — require restarting all instances simultaneously, producing a brief service interruption. This interacts with the emergency patch timeline in a specific way.

from dataclasses import dataclass
from datetime import datetime, timedelta

@dataclass
class DowntimePatchPlan:
    cve_id: str
    estimated_downtime_minutes: float
    maintenance_window_required: bool
    earliest_maintenance_window: datetime
    customer_notice_hours_before: float = 2.0

    def can_deploy_in_window(
        self,
        deadline: datetime,
        current_time: datetime,
    ) -> bool:
        """True if a maintenance window fits within the patch SLA deadline."""
        earliest_deploy = current_time + timedelta(
            hours=self.customer_notice_hours_before
        )
        return earliest_deploy + timedelta(minutes=self.estimated_downtime_minutes) <= deadline

    def customer_downtime_notice(self, window_start: datetime) -> str:
        window_end = window_start + timedelta(minutes=self.estimated_downtime_minutes + 5)
        return (
            f"Planned maintenance: {window_start.strftime('%Y-%m-%d %H:%M UTC')} "
            f"to {window_end.strftime('%H:%M UTC')} (approximately "
            f"{self.estimated_downtime_minutes:.0f} minutes). "
            f"API requests during this window will return 503. "
            f"No data is affected. Service will resume automatically."
        )

    def requires_emergency_notification(
        self,
        deadline: datetime,
        current_time: datetime,
    ) -> bool:
        """
        Returns True if the patch deadline is so tight that the normal 2-hour
        customer notice window cannot be respected.
        Signals that immediate notification (best-effort) is required.
        """
        return not self.can_deploy_in_window(deadline, current_time)

The requires_emergency_notification case — where the patch deadline is so tight that the 2-hour notice window cannot be respected — requires a judgment call from the IC. The options are: deploy with shorter notice (acceptable if the CVE is a confirmed active exploit), accept the SLA miss on the patch deadline in exchange for proper customer notice, or apply a temporary mitigation (WAF rule, feature disable) that buys time for a properly announced maintenance window. The mitigation option should always be evaluated before accepting either trade-off.
Emergency Patch Frequency as a Program Health Metric

The number of emergency patches in a quarter is a program health metric. One or two per quarter may be unavoidable given the pace of CVE disclosure. Five or more suggests systemic problems: outdated dependencies being held too long, a scanning cadence that is too slow to catch vulnerabilities before they reach critical severity, or a dependency portfolio that is too large and poorly curated.
Emergency Patch Count (Quarter)
Interpretation
Response
Good; verify scanning coverage is complete
Confirm no suppressed alerts in scan output
Normal; process is working
Standard post-patch reviews
Elevated; investigate root causes
Review dependency upgrade cadence; reduce version lag
Systemic problem; normal cadence is failing
Audit full dependency portfolio; implement automated upgrade PRs

Automated dependency upgrade pull requests (tools like Dependabot or Renovate) are the structural fix for high emergency patch frequency caused by version lag. They surface available upgrades before they accumulate into a backlog of security debt. The operational cost of reviewing and merging a steady stream of small dependency bumps is far lower than the cost of an emergency patch operation — and it eliminates the version lag that turns HIGH vulnerabilities into CRITICAL ones when an active exploit appears.
Summary

Emergency patch procedures activate on four criteria: active exploit, effective CRITICAL severity, vulnerability on the inference hot path, or suspected data exposure. The command structure assigns IC, patch engineer, security reviewer, and communications lead. Internal updates go to three audience channels at defined cadences. Customer disclosure is mandatory for enterprise accounts within 24 hours of production deploy, explicitly distinguishing server-side patches (no customer action) from SDK vulnerabilities (customer must upgrade). Every emergency patch requires a 48-hour post-patch review covering process gaps, detection source, and specific corrective actions.
